turns.student: How have you been feeling overall lately?
turns.patient: I've been feeling really anxious and worried about my symptoms. To be honest, I'm getting frustrated because they're just getting worse - I'm having bloody stools with mucus, bright red blood mixed with stool, and I'm going to the bathroom way too often, like 8-10 times a day, it's just constant urgency and loose, watery stools.

turns.student: Tell me about your day-to-day — how has your health been in general?
turns.patient: It's been really tough. The diarrhea is constant, so I've had to adjust my work schedule, but it's not easy. I'm a software engineer and we have tight deadlines, but with this frequency, I'm worried about falling behind, and on top of that, I'm just exhausted all the time.

turns.student: You mentioned some health issues. Can you walk me through what happens?
turns.patient: It starts in my lower abdomen, left side mainly, with this crampy pain that can be pretty intense at times. And then I'll feel a sudden urge to go to the bathroom, which is usually right after eating or drinking something. The stools are always loose and watery, and lately, they've been bloody with mucus. It's like my body is constantly reacting this way, even when I'm not eating much.

turns.student: And when these episodes occur, what does your body do?
turns.patient: It feels like my lower abdomen tightens up first, and then I get this overwhelming urge to have a bowel movement. When it happens, it's always loose or watery stools, and sometimes with blood mixed in, which is really scary for me.

turns.student: Let's focus on the episodes. Walk me through one from start to finish.
turns.patient: I get this crampy pain in my lower abdomen, usually on the left side, and it can be quite intense at times. Then, I feel a sudden urge to have a bowel movement, which is usually loose or watery stools, sometimes with blood mixed in with mucus. The sensation of incomplete evacuation after most bowel movements has been really bothering me - it's like my body is never fully emptying, even when I think I've had a complete bowel movement.

turns.student: During an episode, do you notice anything besides the main symptom?
turns.patient: Yes, besides the intense crampy pain and urgency to have a bowel movement, I also feel really fatigued during those episodes. It's like my body is just drained of all its energy, and even simple tasks become super challenging. And sometimes, after an episode, I'll take some loperamide, which gives me a bit of temporary relief from the diarrhea, but it doesn't address the underlying issue.

turns.student: Specifically, any changes in your skin — like sweating or flushing?
turns.patient: Honestly, I haven't really noticed anything out of the ordinary with my skin. My main concerns have been the constant diarrhea, abdominal pain, and fatigue. I've been so focused on managing those symptoms that I haven't paid much attention to any changes in my skin.

turns.student: And your stomach — any queasiness or nausea during these moments?
turns.patient: To be honest, I don't really get queasy or nauseous before or after an episode. It's more like a constant feeling of urgency and discomfort in my lower abdomen that just won't go away, even when I'm not experiencing diarrhea.

turns.student: What about other parts of your body? Any pain, tingling, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: Honestly, no. The crampy pain is mostly confined to my lower abdomen, and like I said, it's usually on the left side. I haven't noticed anything else out of the ordinary in terms of pain or sensations elsewhere in my body. It's really been all about this constant diarrhea and abdominal discomfort for me lately.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've had a low-grade fever, usually around 37.8 to 38.0°C, a few times per week. It's not super high, but it's definitely been persistent and adds to my overall feeling of being unwell.

turns.student: Now let's zoom out. Any swelling you've noticed anywhere on your body?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't really noticed any swelling anywhere on my body that I can think of. The fatigue and constant diarrhea have been my main concerns, so I haven't paid much attention to swelling or anything like that. (pauses) Although, it's worth mentioning, the nocturnal diarrhea has been waking me up 2-3 times a night, which is affecting my sleep significantly.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your weight or appetite?
turns.patient: Yes, I have. To be honest, I've unintentionally lost about 4 kg (9 lbs) over the past 3 weeks due to this constant diarrhea and lack of appetite from it. With my irregular meals, mostly takeout because of work hours, and now this issue with diarrhea, my weight has just dropped significantly without me even realizing it until recently.

turns.student: Let me ask about your medical history. What conditions have you been treated for?
turns.patient: Honestly, I don't really have any significant prior medical conditions that I'm aware of. My only surgery was an appendectomy when I was 16, and everything seemed to heal normally after that. I've always tried to take care of myself, but this current situation is just so frustrating because it's affecting my daily life in such a big way.

turns.student: Any medications you're taking? Are you taking them consistently?
turns.patient: To be honest, I'm self-medicating with loperamide for the diarrhea, but I've only been using it for about 2 weeks now. It gives me some temporary relief, but it's not addressing the underlying issue at all, and I feel like I'm just covering up symptoms rather than treating the problem itself.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle. How active are you?
turns.patient: To be honest, my activity level has decreased significantly due to this constant diarrhea and fatigue. As a software engineer at a startup, I'm already working long hours, sometimes 60+ hour weeks, with high stress levels, but now I'm just feeling exhausted all the time. I try to stay active by going for short walks during my lunch break or doing some light stretching at home, but it's hard to keep up with my usual routine when I'm constantly running to the bathroom and feeling drained.

turns.student: And your diet — what does a typical day of eating look like?
turns.patient: To be honest, my diet has been pretty irregular lately due to my long work hours. Mostly takeout for convenience, which isn't great I know, but it's just easier with the schedule I have. Sometimes I grab something quick at a food truck or restaurant near the office, other times I'll order delivery from a favorite place. It's not like I'm eating a lot, though - my appetite has actually decreased because of the diarrhea and fatigue. But when I do eat, it's often just something fast and easy to get back to work quickly.

turns.student: How about alcohol or smoking?
turns.patient: I drink 2-3 beers most weeknights, but over the past month, that number has increased to 4-5 per night due to stress from my symptoms and work. I'm not a smoker.

turns.student: Now family history. Any medical problems run in your family?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30, which is actually one of the reasons I've been worried about my own symptoms lately - it just feels like a possible connection to what I'm experiencing.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious or chronic conditions?
turns.patient: Yes. My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. That's been on my mind lately because of the similarities between her symptoms and mine - especially the persistent diarrhea and abdominal pain.

turns.student: We've covered a lot. Is there anything we haven't discussed that you think is relevant?
turns.patient: To be honest, I feel like we're still missing some important pieces. My job as a software engineer is extremely stressful, and the constant diarrhea is not only affecting my work performance but also my personal life. I'm starting to feel really overwhelmed by this whole situation, and it's taking a toll on my mental health as well. I've noticed that I've been drinking more beer than usual to try to cope with the stress, which isn't helping the situation either. And, if I'm being completely honest, I've been feeling pretty frustrated with my healthcare system because it feels like no one has been able to give me a clear answer about what's going on or how to fix it.
